ARGUED AND THE COURT ADOPTED ITS TENTATIVE RULING AS FOLLOWS: MOTION DENIED. LONG VS. CENTURY INDEMNITY CO. (2008) 163 CAL.APP. 4TH 1460 IS DISTINGUISHABLE IN THAT THE INSURER PROVIDED A DEFENSE AND DID NOT BREACH ITS DUTY TO DEFEND. COMPULINK MANAGEMENT CENTER, INC. VS. ST. PAUL FIRE AND MARINE INSURANCE CO. (2008) 169 CAL.APP. 4TH 289 IS SIMILARLY DISTINGUISHABLE AND DID NOT ADDRESS THE SITUATION RAISED IN THE INSTANT ACTION. SUPERIOR COURT (2010) DAILY JOURNAL D.A.R. 4406) DEFENDANT'S REMEDY IS TO PURSUE THE REIMBURSEMENT CLAIM. NO AUTHORITY SUPPORTS THE POSTING OF A BOND. (PREVAILING PARTY TO PREPARE A FORM OF ORDER.) Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Discovery From Cross-Complainant Hartford Casualty Insurance Company, Or In The Alternative, Motion In Limine To Preclude Evidence
Off Calendar Oct-02-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Attorney, Stephen M. Hayes of the law offices of Hayes Scott Bonino Ellingson McLay LLP (650-637-9100) appears as counsel for defendant/cross-complainant/opposing party, Hartford Casualty Insurance Co.

Attorneys James Fogelman and Jessica N. Lemaux of the law offices of Gibson Dunn & Crutcher LLP (415-393-8272) appear as counsels for cross-defendant/moving party, Squire Patton Boggs (US) LLP

Court modifies, corrects an error in the tentative ruling with both sides present. The cause is argued and submitted by both sides as set forth in the transcript of the certified shorthand reporter. The Court confirms the tentative ruling with modification.

CROSS DEFENDANT SQUIRE PATTON BOGGS (US) LLP's Motion To Compel Discovery From Cross-Complainant Hartford Casualty Insurance Company, Or In The Alternative, Motion In Limine To Preclude Evidence is GRANTED in part. Hartford's responses do not meet the "complete and straight-forward" standard under the Code, improperly reference other documents, and, in the case of its responses to RFAs, are noncompliant with CCP 2033.2022(b). For example, Hartford's amended response to Interrogatory No. 78 that, "The fees overall for the type of case (corporate divorce), the value of the company, the matters actually in dispute, and the number of witnesses, among other factors, were excessive."- is not complete or straight-forward. If Hartford cannot answer an interrogatory completely, it shall answer it to the extent possible. Similarly, Hartford's responses to RFAs are inadequate. For example, its response to No. 296 that "The ENFORCEMENT ORDER expressly stated that Hartford had the right to seek reimbursement ' Hartford abided by the ENFORCEMENT order ' Hartford refers Squire to the opinion and testimony of its expert '"-is not complete or straight-forward, or compliant with C.C.P. 2033.220(b). Also, referencing other documents, especially without summarizing their contents, is improper; the answer, by itself, should be fully responsive. If Hartford's position is that the overall fees for this matter were not objectively reasonable or necessary, then it responses should so state in a manner that is complete and straight-forward. To the extent Hartford contends that specific elements of the fees were improper, its responses shall identify the specific fee elements it regards as unnecessary, unreasonable, or not beneficial to the insureds. In either case, SPB is entitled to discover what contentions Hartford will advance at trial-including any specific fee elements it regards as unnecessary, unreasonable, or not beneficial to the insureds-and on 5/8/2017 the court ruled that SPB may pursue these topics through written interrogatories. This does not necessarily implicate a "line-by-line" analysis of its bills, as Hartford argues. Hartford shall provide verified, Code-complaint responses to Interrogatories Nos. 78-79, RFAs Nos. 165-314, and Form Interrogatory No. 17.1, within 20 days. The prevailing party shall submit a [proposed] written order that tracks the Court's ruling, verbatim and that complies with CRC 3.1312 and do so no later than October 3, 2017.

Judge: Charlene P. Kiesselbach; Clerk: J. Rios Merida; Reporter: Reyes Hunter, CSR No. 6576, e-mail: rhunter@sftc.org. (L&M302)

Notice Of Application And Application For Order Sealing Record
Off Calendar Jun-01-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearance on this matter. The Court adopted its tentative ruling.
DEFENDANT HARTFORD CASUALTY INSURANCE COMPANY'S Application For Order Sealing Record
Hartford seeks an order sealing its settlement agreement with J.R. Marketing, LLC; Jane E. Ratto; Robert E. Ratto; and Penelope Kane on the basis that the agreement contains a confidentiality clause preventing disclosure of its terms and content. Hartford relies primarily on the third circuit opinion in Publickler Industries, Inc. v. Cohen (3rd Cir 1984) 733 F.2d 1059 as authority for the proposition that disclosure of the settlement agreement terms would "deprive the litigant of its right to enforce a legal obligation." (Hartford's Memo of P's and A's p. 2, line 11.)California courts have not given Publicker such a broad reading as proposed by Hartford. [A] thorough reading of all of the Publicker opinion makes it clear that the Third Circuit requires more than a mere agreement of the parties to seal documents filed in a public courtroom' in addition, there must be a no less restrictive way to protect that governmental interest (ibid.); in order to close a civil proceeding there must be evidence of "serious injury"; and the serious injury must be shown with specificity. (Id. at p. 1071.) Other courts applying Publicker have emphasized that closure or sealing can only occur under the Third Circuit rule when there has been a specific showing of serious injury. Universal City Studios, Inc. v. Superior Court (2003) 110 Cal.App.4th 1273, 1281-1282. California Rule of Court 2.550(d) allows sealing of a record if "(1) There exists an overriding interest that overcomes t he right of public access to the record; (2) The overriding interest supports sealing the record; (3) A substantial probability exists that the overriding interest will be prejudiced if the record is not sealed'" Here, Hartford has failed to identify any overriding interest other than the right of a litigant to enforce a legal obligation. Assuming this interest by itself is indeed overriding, which is questionable, Hartford has failed to show a substantial probability of prejudice if the record is not sealed. Hartford claims it would be prejudiced "in being forced to forefeit [sic] the mutual protections of the Settlement Agreement, and by possibly undermining any future settlement negotiations, preventing resolution of this and other future litigation." (Hartford's Memo of P's and A's p. 2, lines 18-20.) These conclusory statements are not supported by any facts or evidence. As in Universal Studios, the settlement agreement is a routine settlement document. Defendant has presented no evidence that disclosure of any of the substantive provisions of the settlement agreement "will prejudice any legitimate confidential business practice." Id. at 1284. Accordingly, the application to seal the settlement agreement is DENIED. Prevailing party to prepare a form of order and to comply with CRC 3.1312. Judge: Stephen Murphy; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Not Reported. =302/sm

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Stay Proceedings
Off Calendar Jun-03-2016 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

No appearance was made by any Party, Counsel or Interested Person.

The Court noted that pursuant to the e-mail received from Counsel for Defendant Hartford Casualty Insurance Company, the proposed order has been approved as to form by Mr. Kahn (Counsel for Cross-Defendant Squire Pattons Boggs LLP). The Court adopted its tentative ruling. CROSS-DEFENDANT SQUIRE PATTON BOGGS LLP's Motion To Stay Proceedings is DENIED. Squire seeks to stay proceedings against it pending resolution of the appeal in J.R. Marketing v. Hartford Casualty Insurance Company, Case No. A141799, currently pending in Division 3 of the First District Court of Appeal ("the Appeal"). The court finds that, on this record, Squire has not demonstrated that a stay of the proceedings against it is in the interest of justice or will promote judicial economy. (See Freiberg v. City of Mission Viejo (1995) 33 Cal.App.4th 1484, 1489.) Nor has Squire demonstrated that allowing proceedings against it at this time will result in "entirely unnecessary" litigation or seriously risk "unseemly conflict" between decisions. (See Caiafa Prof'l Law Corp. v. State Farm Fire & Cas. Co. (1993) 15 Cal.App.4th 800, 807.) The framework for Hartford's claims against Squire is set out in Hartford Cas. Insur. Co. v. J. R. Marketing, LLC (2015) 61 Cal.4th 988 and there is no substantial showing that the results of the Appeal will have any significant effect on this framework. While the Appeal could affect the scope of trial or the relief available, this possibility is not cause for delay of pre-trial proceedings such as fact discovery, expert discovery, and pretrial motion practice. These steps should occur now so that the claims against Squire are ready--or closer to ready--for trial when the Appeal is resolved. At this time and on this record, the court finds further delay of these claims, which have been pending since 2006, unjustified. This order is without prejudice to renewal of the motion at a later date if there is a substantial change in circumstances that would merit a stay of the proceedings against Squire. Order signed.

Judge: Joseph Quinn. Clerk: Rosie Noguera. Reporter: Sherry Sawyer, CSR #5976. (302/HK)

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
Off Calendar Dec-16-2011 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: DEFENDANT HARTFORD CASUALTY INSURANCE COMPANY'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT AND SUMMARY ADJUDICATION OF ISSUES.
Appearances: Michelle M. Full and Ethan A. Miller of Squire Sanders (954.0500) for plaintiff and responding party; Catherine L. Rivard of Mendes & Mount LLP (213.955.7700) for defendant and moving party.
This matter was heard at 1:30 pm. Argued and the court adopted its tentative ruling as follows: summary adjudication granted as to all four of the issues. Notwithstanding the enormous quantity of paper filed by the parties, each issue, at its core, presents a question of law as to which there is no dispute regarding the material facts. As to issues one and three, Hartford has no duty to post the appeal bond or defend the Nevada action because, as a matter of law, the claims in the Marin interpleader action and the Nevada action are neither covered nor potentially covered by the Hartford policies. As to issue two, as a matter of conflicts law, the law of Virginia applies and thus Hartford has no duty to pay for independent counsel in the Virginia action. As to issue four, as a matter of law, Mr. DeMartinis is neither a manager nor an employee, as those terms are used in the JRMLLC policy, and thus Hartford has no duty to defend him. Prevailing party to submit a proposed form of order. (302/HEK) Judge: Harold Kahn, Reporter: Sonia Wong, CSR #11288

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED CROSS COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Sep-01-2011 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
CROSS-DEFENDANTS J.R. MARKETING, LLC, JANE E. RATTO, ROBERT E. RATTO, PENELOPE A. KANE, LENORE DEMARTINIS, GERMAIN DEMARTINIS, SCOTT HARRINGTON AND SQUIRE, SANDERS & DEMPSEY (US) LLP'S JOINT AND SEVERAL DEMURRER TO HARTFORD CASUALTY INSURANCE CO.'S FIRST AMENDED CROSS-COMPLAINT.

Appearances: Ethan A. Miller, Esq. 954-0288 & Michelle M. Full, Esq. 954-0366 of Squire, Sanders & Dempsey (US) LLP for moving parties; Ira G. Greenberg, Esq. of Edwards Angell Palmer & Dodge LLP 212/912-2756 for opposing party Hartford Casualty Insurance Company; Paul Vacquier, Esq. of Edgerton & Weaver, LLP 310/937-2066 for Bonafide Insurance Services, Inc. (appearing via Court Call).

RULING: Argued and the Court took the matter under submission.
After hearing, the Court ruled as follows: The Court adopts its tentative ruling as final. Following oral argument, the Court read the cases presented by counsel during argument and reread the Buss v. Superior Court decision. THE RIGHT TO REIMBURSEMENT ARISES FROM THE INSURER-INSURED RELATIONSHIP. BUSS V. SUP. CRT. (1997) 16 CAL.4TH 35. In addition, the Court found the Jackson v. Rogers & Wells case particularly instructive and on point as to the issue under consideration in this case as to whether Hartford could pursue claims for reimbursement, unjust enrichment, accounting and rescission against their insured's Cumis counsel. The Court finds the legal analysis and policy considerations, protecting the integrity of the attorney-client relationship between the insured and their Cumis Counsel, as explained in Jackson to be applicable to this case and persuasive. Accordingly, the non-insured Defendants' Demurrer to the reimbursement and rescission causes of action in the Amended Cross-Complaint are sustained without leave to amend.
The Insured Defendant's Demurrer to the reimbursement and rescission causes of action are overruled.
The Demurrer to the unjust enrichment and accounting causes of action are sustained without leave to amend as to all defendants. Defendant's remedy is to pursue the reimbursement cause of action against the insured. Moving party will prepare a proposed form of order. (302/LMG) Judge: Loretta M. Giorgi, Reporter: Karla Ellis-Davis, CSR #12998

Notice Of Motion And Motion By Hartford Casualty Insurance Company To Dissolve Or Modify The "Enforcement Order" Entered By Sep-27-06
Off Calendar Apr-16-2010 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: CROSS COMPLAINANT HARTFORD CASUALTY INSURANCE COMPANY Motion by Hartford Casualty Insurance Company to Dissolve or Modify the "Enforcement Order" Entered by September 27, 2006. Appearances: Ethan A. Miller, Esq. & Barry D. Brown, Esq. of Squire Sanders LLP (415) 954-0200, appearing for Plaintiffs and opposing party; Dean B. Herman, of Mendes (213) 955-7700, appearing for Cross Complainant and Moving party. Argued and the Court adopted its tentative ruling as follows: Motion denied. Long vs. Century Indemnity Co. (2008) 163 Cal.App. 4th 1460 is distinguishable in that the insurer provided a defense and did not breach its duty to defend. Compulink Management Center, Inc. vs. St. Paul Fire and Marine Insurance Co. (2008) 169 Cal.App. 4th 289 is similarly distinguishable and did not address the situation raised in the instant action. Where in the insurer breaches its duty to defend it forfeits the protection of Civil Code section 2860. (See Intergulf Dev't Co. vs. Superior Court (2010) Daily Journal D.A.R. 4406) Defendant's remedy is to pursue the reimbursement claim. No authority supports the posting of a bond. (Prevailing party to prepare a form of order.) (302/CWW)Judge: Charlotte Walter Woolard; Court Reporter: Carlito Mandia, CSR # 9689

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel James J. Jennings To Provide Further Responses To Deposition Questions And The Production Of Documents And Request For Sanction
Off Calendar Jul-26-2006 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES:
Leni Masserman for defendant Hartfor Casualty Insurance, Co.

NO OTHER APPEARANCE. TENTATIVE RULING ADOPTED. Plaintiff J. R. Marketing's motion to compel further responses at deposition is granted.
Question #1
Granted. However, a motion for a protective order pursuant to CCP Section 2025.420(b) may be appropriate if counsel for plaintiff engages in duplicative questioning as to matters where the information may be obtained by alternative methods. The basis for the instruction to the witness to refuse to answer the question was improper.
Questions #2 - 4
Granted. Notwithstanding the previous order relating to defendant's motion for a protective order, instructing the witness to refuse to answer a question based on alleged violations of the California Rules of Professional Responsibility was improper. COMMISSIONER: BRUCE E. CHAN

Plaintiff J. R. Marketing Llc'S Motion To Compel Production Of Documents
Off Calendar Jul-26-2006 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES:
Leni Masserman for defendant Hartford Casualty Insurance Company
NO OTHER APPEARANCE. TENTATIVE RULING ADOPTED. Plaintiff J. R. Marketing's motion to compel production of documents at deposition is granted in part and denied in part. The motion is moot as to requests 1 - 5, 6, 7, and 12. The motion is withdrawn as to request 8.
The motion is granted as to requests 9 and 10 subject to the following limitations: claims files in California from January 1, 2001 to January 1, 2006 that were the subject of litigation alleging that defendant improperly denied a defense under the advertising injury / personal injury coverage section of a general liability policy where some, but not all, causes of action are potentially covered. Defendant may produce evidence of the earliest and latest ten files for the types of claims at issue. Defendant shall redact the name, address, telephone, social security number, or any other personal identifying information of insured persons as well as any information subject to a claim of attorney-client or attorney work product privilege.
The motion is denied as to request 11 at this time.
The motion is granted as to request 13 and 14. If defendant objects to production of particular documents based on the attorney-client or attorney work product privilege, said documents shall be identified with particularity in a privilege log.
The parties are ordered to meet and confer as to resuming the deposition. The deposition shall take place at such time as to afford defendant a reasonable opportunity to produce the responsive documents at least three business days in advance of the deposition. COMMISSIONER: BRUCE E. CHAN
